evidence: Allegion Ventures announced a second fund with an additional allocation of $100 million to focus on investing in technologies like artificial intelligence, video monitoring, machine learning and cybersecurity
Allegion Ventures to invest in and help accelerate the growth of companies that have innovative, digital-first technologies
we expect continued growth in connected security products and solutions as end-users continue to adopt newer technologies, including mobile solutions and artificial intelligence
continual development of new technologies, such as artificial intelligence and machine learning, by existing and new competitors...could adversely affect our ability to sustain operating margins

AI enabled methods, including automated vulnerability discovery, automated credential stuffing, deepfake assisted social engineering and other generative AI techniques, could make attacks more successful and harder to detect
our own or third party use of AI could introduce new classes of vulnerabilities...increase our remediation and compliance costs, harm our reputation and subject us to regulatory or legal liability
The use of AI by our stakeholders, including vendors and customers, may also introduce new vulnerabilities into our ecosystem

capex_mentions.quote: additional allocation of $100 million to focus on investing in technologies like artificial intelligence, video monitoring, machine learning and cybersecurity
capex_mentions.context: Allegion Ventures second fund announced December 2021, focused on AI and related technologies

limitations: The $100 million Allegion Ventures fund was announced in December 2021; it appears in this 2026 filing as historical context, not a new commitment — investment intensity score reflects named program with disclosed amount but uncertain current deployment status.
AI narrative concreteness is limited: excerpts describe AI primarily as a market trend and competitive threat rather than detailing Allegion's own deployed AI use cases or products.

Risk disclosure scored 4 based on specific mechanisms (deepfake social engineering, automated vulnerability discovery, third-party ecosystem vulnerabilities, compliance/reputational impact) but no quantified risk exposure or formal AI governance body is named beyond the CISO cybersecurity oversight role.
